arguments.name: explore-aot
arguments.prompt:
Explore the project in /tmp/sv-0c263b653aa348f29b59b2a4068d5a69/before/ to identify all AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation compatibility issues in a .NET project.

AOT compatibility issues typically include:
1. Missing `[JsonSerializable]` attributes on serializer contexts
2. Use of reflection-based serialization (e.g., `JsonSerializer.Serialize/Deserialize` without a source-generated context)
3. Missing `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` annotations
4. Use of `Type.MakeGenericType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, or other reflection APIs without proper annotations
5. The csproj may need `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` 
6. Source generators may be needed for JSON serialization
7. `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and custom serialization patterns that use reflection

Specifically:
1. Read the .csproj file and all .cs files to understand the project structure
2. Search for any JSON serialization patterns (JsonSerializer, JsonSerializerOptions, System.Text.Json usage)
3. Search for reflection usage (Type.GetType, Activator.CreateInstance, MakeGenericType, etc.)
4. Search for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and related serialization interfaces
5. Look at model classes that implement serialization
6. Check for any `JsonConverter` implementations
7. Look for dynamic type loading or assembly scanning
8. Look at the Generated/ directories under Common/, Resources/, ManagementGroup/ for auto-generated model code
9. Check the Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs file
10. Look for any `BinaryData`, `JsonElement`, `Utf8JsonWriter`, `Utf8JsonReader` usage patterns

Report ALL files and specific code patterns that would need changes for AOT compatibility. Include file paths and line numbers.

Explore the project in /tmp/sv-0c263b653aa348f29b59b2a4068d5a69/before/ to identify all AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation compatibility issues in a .NET project.

AOT compatibility issues typically include:
1. Missing `[JsonSerializable]` attributes on serializer contexts
2. Use of reflection-based serialization (e.g., `JsonSerializer.Serialize/Deserialize` without a source-generated context)
3. Missing `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` annotations
4. Use of `Type.MakeGenericType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, or other reflection APIs without proper annotations
5. The csproj may need `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` 
6. Source generators may be needed for JSON serialization
7. `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and custom serialization patterns that use reflection

Specifically:
1. Read the .csproj file and all .cs files to understand the project structure
2. Search for any JSON serialization patterns (JsonSerializer, JsonSerializerOptions, System.Text.Json usage)
3. Search for reflection usage (Type.GetType, Activator.CreateInstance, MakeGenericType, etc.)
4. Search for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and related serialization interfaces
5. Look at model classes that implement serialization
6. Check for any `JsonConverter` implementations
7. Look for dynamic type loading or assembly scanning
8. Look at the Generated/ directories under Common/, Resources/, ManagementGroup/ for auto-generated model code
9. Check the Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs file
10. Look for any `BinaryData`, `JsonElement`, `Utf8JsonWriter`, `Utf8JsonReader` usage patterns

Report ALL files and specific code patterns that would need changes for AOT compatibility. Include file paths and line numbers.
